skill.name: Workflow Patterns
skill.description: Use this skill when implementing tasks according to Conductor's TDD workflow, handling phase checkpoints, managing git commits for tasks, or understanding the verification protocol.
skill.summary: Guide for TDD task implementation with phase checkpoints, git commit management, and verification protocols.
skill.author: sickn33

security_audit.summary: Static analysis detected 80 potential security issues in resources/implementation-playbook.md, all of which are false positives. The file contains Markdown documentation with bash code examples for educational purposes. The external_commands patterns are git command examples in code blocks (lines 25-571), not executable code. The weak_crypto findings are documentation text, not cryptographic implementations. This skill contains only documentation and no executable code, posing no security risk.

medium_findings.title: External Command Patterns in Documentation
medium_findings.description: Static scanner detected Ruby/shell backtick execution patterns at 65 locations in resources/implementation-playbook.md. These are all bash code examples within Markdown code blocks demonstrating git commands (git commit, git notes, git diff, pytest, etc.) for educational purposes. No executable code exists.

content.value_statement: Struggling with disorganized development workflow and poor test coverage. This skill provides a structured TDD framework with phase checkpoints, git commit management, and verification protocols to ensure code quality throughout implementation.
actual_capabilities: Implement phase checkpoints with verification protocols
Manage git commits with structured commit messages and git notes
Enforce quality assurance gates (coverage, style, linting, security)
Handle deviations from original plans systematically
Create verification checklists for automated and manual testing
limitations: Requires git repository to utilize commit and checkpoint features
Focused on TDD methodology - not suitable for non-test-driven workflows
Documentation-only skill - does not execute commands or enforce rules automatically
Designed for Conductor framework - may need adaptation for other workflows

use_cases.title: Implement New Feature with TDD
use_cases.description: Developer implements a new user authentication feature following the red-green-refactor cycle, with proper test coverage and git commits at each step.

use_cases.title: Establish Phase Checkpoints
use_cases.description: Team lead creates verification checkpoints at the end of each development phase to ensure quality gates are met before proceeding.

use_cases.title: Track Implementation Progress
use_cases.description: Developer tracks task completion by updating plan.md with commit SHAs and maintaining traceability from planning to code.

prompt_templates.title: Start TDD Task Implementation
prompt_templates.prompt: I'm starting Task 2.1: Implement user validation. Help me follow the TDD workflow to write failing tests first, then implement the minimum code to pass, and refactor for clarity.
prompt_templates.scenario: Beginning a new task from plan.md

prompt_templates.title: Create Phase Checkpoint
prompt_templates.prompt: I've completed all tasks in Phase 1. Help me create a checkpoint commit with verification: run full test suite, check coverage above 80%, and generate manual verification checklist.
prompt_templates.scenario: Completing a phase and need checkpoint verification

prompt_templates.title: Format Structured Git Commit
prompt_templates.prompt: Help me create a structured commit message for Task 2.1 completion following the format: type(scope): subject, body with bullet points, and footer with task/track references.
prompt_templates.scenario: Committing completed task with proper format

prompt_templates.title: Handle Implementation Deviation
prompt_templates.prompt: During implementation I discovered we need a different approach than planned. Help me document this deviation properly in plan.md and tech-stack.md.
prompt_templates.scenario: Deviating from original plan during implementation

output_examples.input: Help me create a checkpoint commit after completing Phase 1
Guide me through the TDD workflow for Task 2.1
output: Step 1: Select next task - Task 2.1 marked
Step 2: Mark as in-progress [~] in plan.md
Step 4: GREEN - Implement minimum code (validate_email method)
Step 5: REFACTOR - Extract patterns, improve naming
Step 9: Attach git notes with summary
Step 10: Update plan.md with commit SHA

best_practices: Always write failing tests first (RED) before implementation (GREEN) - never skip the RED phase
Create atomic commits - one logical change per commit with tests passing after every commit
Wait for explicit user approval before proceeding past phase checkpoints - do not skip verification gates
Update plan.md immediately after completing each task with the commit SHA for traceability
anti_patterns: Writing implementation code before tests - this violates TDD principles and reduces test effectiveness
Batching multiple tasks into one commit - this breaks traceability and makes semantic reversion difficult
Proceeding to next phase without checkpoint approval - this bypasses quality gates and risks accumulating technical debt
Updating plan.md only after multiple tasks - this loses traceability and makes progress auditing difficult

faq.question: What is the RED-GREEN-REFACTOR cycle?
faq.answer: RED means write failing tests first. GREEN means write minimum code to pass tests. REFACTOR means improve code structure while keeping tests green. This cycle ensures tests drive development and code stays clean.

faq.question: Why do I need to record commit SHAs in plan.md?
faq.answer: Recording commit SHAs creates traceability from plan to code. It enables semantic revert operations, progress auditing, and helps you understand which code implements which specific task.

faq.question: Can I proceed to the next phase without checkpoint approval?
faq.answer: No. Checkpoint approval is a critical quality gate. Proceeding without approval risks accumulating undetected issues. Always wait for explicit approval before moving to the next phase.

faq.question: What should I do if implementation deviates from the plan?
faq.answer: Document the deviation in plan.md with the reason and impact. Update tech-stack.md if dependencies changed. Update spec.md if requirements changed. This maintains traceability of decisions.

faq.question: Why use git notes instead of putting everything in the commit message?
faq.answer: Git notes preserve rich context without cluttering commit messages. They enable semantic queries across commits and support track-based operations while keeping commits concise and scannable.

faq.question: What is the 80% coverage target and why is it important?
faq.answer: The 80% coverage target ensures comprehensive testing of new code. It balances thoroughness with pragmatism - 100% is often not cost-effective, but below 80% risks missing critical paths.
